title: Local LLM Framework DNS Query
spl:
`sysmon`
    EventCode=22
    QueryName IN (
        "*huggingface*",
        "*ollama*",
        "*jan.ai*",
        "*gpt4all*",
        "*nomic*",
        "*koboldai*",
        "*lmstudio*",
        "*modelscope*",
        "*civitai*",
        "*oobabooga*",
        "*replicate*",
        "*anthropic*",
        "*openai*",
        "*openrouter*",
        "*api.openrouter*",
        "*aliyun*",
        "*alibabacloud*",
        "*dashscope.aliyuncs*"
    )
    NOT Image IN (
        "*\\MsMpEng.exe",
        "C:\\ProgramData\\*",
        "C:\\Windows\\System32\\*",
        "C:\\Windows\\SysWOW64\\*"
    )
    | stats count
        min(_time) as firstTime
        max(_time) as lastTime
        by src Image process_name QueryName query_count answer answer_count reply_code_id vendor_product
    | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)`
    | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
    | `local_llm_framework_dns_query_filter`
description: This detection identifies Local LLM Framework DNS Query. It supports security monitoring and incident response. Part of Splunk Security Essentials (ESCU) where applicable.
value: This detection identifies Local LLM Framework DNS Query. It supports security monitoring and incident response. Part of Splunk Security Essentials (ESCU) where applicable.
implementation: ESCU Hunting detection — not designed for automated alerting. Run ad-hoc or on a low-frequency schedule from Splunk Enterprise Security for analyst-driven investigation. Requires Sysmon EventID 22 ingested and CIM-normalized.
cimSpl: | tstats `summariesonly` count from datamodel=Network_Resolution.DNS by DNS.src DNS.query span=1h | sort -count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable Data Model Acceleration for the CIM `Network_Resolution.DNS` dataset; otherwise `cimSpl` tstats may return no rows.
knownFalsePositives: Security research, package managers, new SaaS rollouts, and browser/CDN look-alikes can hit suspicious name lists. Allowlist vetted resolvers, dev sandboxes, and known collaboration apps after review.
detailedImplementation:
Enterprise Security Detection Rule

"Local LLM Framework DNS Query" is a Hunting detection designed for analyst-driven threat hunting rather than automated alerting, sourced from the Splunk Enterprise Security Content Update (ESCU). Hunting detections are not intended for automated alerting. They are run on-demand or on a low-frequency schedule by security analysts investigating specific threat hypotheses.

Prerequisites

• Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x or later with the ES Content Update (ESCU) app installed and up to date.
• Data sources: Sysmon EventID 22. Must be ingested into Splunk and normalized to the Common Information Model (CIM) via the appropriate Technology Add-on.
• MITRE ATT&CK: T1590. Review the ATT&CK matrix for adjacent techniques to identify detection coverage gaps in your environment.

Deployment

1. In Enterprise Security, navigate to Configure → Content → Content Management.
2. Search for "Local LLM Framework DNS Query" or filter by Analytic Story.
3. Hunting detections are typically left disabled for automated scheduling. Instead, run them on-demand from the Search bar or schedule at low frequency (daily or weekly).
4. Review results manually — hunting detections cast a wider net and expect analyst judgment to separate signal from noise.
5. When results warrant further investigation, create a Notable Event manually or initiate your incident response workflow.

Tuning and False Positive Management

Known false positives for this detection: Security research, package managers, new SaaS rollouts, and browser/CDN look-alikes can hit suspicious name lists. Allowlist vetted resolvers, dev sandboxes, and known collaboration apps after review.

• Hunting detections are expected to produce broader result sets that require analyst interpretation. Focus on refining the search scope (time range, specific hosts or users) rather than eliminating all noise.
• Maintain a hunting journal documenting hypotheses tested, results found, and detection improvements identified.
• Share high-fidelity findings with the detection engineering team to convert recurring hunting patterns into automated TTP or Anomaly detections.

Analyst Response Workflow

When reviewing Hunting results:

1. Run the search manually or review scheduled results. Evaluate each returned event against your threat hypothesis — not every result indicates compromise.
2. Cross-reference findings with current threat intelligence, recent security advisories, and outputs from other detection sources.
3. If suspicious activity is confirmed, create a Notable Event or escalate directly to your incident response process with the supporting evidence.
4. Document the hunting exercise: hypothesis, data sources queried, time range, findings, and any detection engineering improvements identified.

Validation

**Validate in production**

• Compare a sample of events with the source-of-truth console for the data source (cloud admin portal, device UI, or directory) covering the same time range.
• If you route findings through Enterprise Security, check the **Incident Review** queue and correlation search health so notables and drilldowns line up with what you see in search.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an eye on Local LLM Framework Query so unusual activity is visible early — before it spreads or hides in normal noise.
